source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 6.1-6.2 vi command & operating mode (pp. 83-86)

questions.q: Why is VI considered a crucial tool for an administrator?
options: It is a text-mode editor present on every Linux/Unix system, so it is available even during a system failure
It is the fastest graphical editor available
It is the only editor that can open configuration files
It automatically saves work every few seconds
questions.why: VI is entirely text-mode and universal across distributions and Unix, so it works even when little else does (e.g. a backup shell after a failure).

questions.q: Open the file /home/rockstar/file in the VI editor.
questions.why: vi <file> opens it in command mode; if the file does not exist, VI shows a blank page and takes that name when you first save.

questions.q: During an editing session, when is the ORIGINAL file actually modified?
options: Only when you save; until then all changes stay in a buffer file
Continuously, as you type each character
Automatically every 30 seconds
As soon as you switch to insert mode
questions.why: VI records changes in a buffer; the original file is untouched until you save, and there is no periodic backup - save regularly.

questions.q: What does vim add over classic VI?
options: The same interface and functions plus improvements such as syntax highlighting
A completely different, mouse-only interface
It removes command mode
It can only edit shell scripts
questions.why: vim keeps VI's interface and functions but adds improvements like syntax highlighting, useful for scripts and config files.

questions.q: What are VI's three working modes?
options: Command mode, Insert mode, and Ex mode
Read mode, Write mode, and Delete mode
Normal mode, Visual mode, and Print mode
Text mode, Binary mode, and Safe mode
questions.why: VI alternates between command and insert modes; the third, ex mode, is a footer command mode inherited from an older editor.

questions.q: Which mode is VI in when it starts, and how do you return to it from any other mode?
options: It starts in command mode; press Esc to return to it
It starts in insert mode; press Enter to return
It starts in ex mode; press : to return
It starts in insert mode; press Esc to return
questions.why: VI starts in command mode, the default; the Esc key always brings you back to it.

questions.q: In insert mode, is text written straight into the file?
options: No - it goes into a memory buffer and only takes effect when the file is saved
Yes - each keystroke edits the file on disk immediately
Only if the file already existed
Only in vim, not in classic VI
questions.why: Insert mode edits a buffer in memory; the changes become effective on disk only when you save.
